content.problemStatement:
An array BBB of length MMM is said to be good if no two elements from different indices sum to 444.

That is, BBB is good if for every pair (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) satisfying 1‚â§i<j‚â§M1 \le i \lt j \le M1‚â§i<j‚â§M, we have

(Bi+Bj)‚âÝ4(B_i + B_j) \ne 4(Bi‚Äã+Bj‚Äã)ÓÄÝ=4.

You are given an array AAA of length NNN such that 1‚â§Ai‚â§31 \le A_i \le 31‚â§Ai‚Äã‚â§3 for each index iii.

You would like to make AAA a good array.

To achieve this, you can repeatedly choose a single element of AAA and delete it.

What's the **minimum** number of deletions needed to make AAA a good array?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚Äî the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã ‚Äî the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the minimum number of element deletions needed to make AAA a good array.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array A=[1,2,2]A = [1, 2, 2]A=[1,2,2] is not good, because A2+A3=4A_2 + A_3 = 4A2‚Äã+A3‚Äã=4.
One deletion is enough to make it good: for example, we can delete the second element and obtain the array A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2], which is good.
Test case 222: The array A=[1,1]A = [1, 1]A=[1,1] is already good, since the only pair of elements it has sums up to 222.
Test case 333: The array A=[2,3,1]A = [2, 3, 1]A=[2,3,1] is not good, because A2+A3=4A_2 + A_3 = 4A2‚Äã+A3‚Äã=4.
Deleting either A2A_2A2‚Äã or A3A_3A3‚Äã will make it good, so one deletion is enough.
Test case 444: The array A=[2,3,1,3,2]A = [2, 3, 1, 3, 2]A=[2,3,1,3,2] is not good.
Two deletions are required to make it good: for example, the third element and last element can be deleted to make the array [2,3,3][2, 3, 3][2,3,3], which is good.
It can be proved that AAA cannot become good after a single deletion.
